name: Fraud Platform
mission: Protect every transaction. We own the real-time fraud-scoring path that gates ~$2M/day in payments across 14 markets — fast enough to add <50ms p99, smart enough to learn from every chargeback, and reliable enough to fail safe when upstream payment processors hiccup.

currentProjects.title: Q1 transactions table partitioning
currentProjects.description: Partitioning the high-volume transactions table by month to keep query latency flat as data grows. Covers planner-stat refresh cadence and a zero-downtime cutover plan.
currentProjects.problem: The transactions table is growing faster than planner statistics can keep up, which makes fraud-rule lookups unpredictable during peak payment volume.
approach: Partition transactions by month with a compatibility view so application code keeps the same read path.
Backfill partitions in batches behind read-only verification queries before moving writes.
Add planner-stat refresh checks and dashboard alerts for partitions with stale estimates.
successMetrics: p99 fraud lookup latency stays under 85ms during peak volume
Zero customer-visible downtime during partition cutover
Rollback path verified in staging and production shadow run
milestones: Backfill the last 18 months of transaction history
Retire the unpartitioned table after validation
risks: Long-running backfill jobs could compete with fraud scoring reads.
Queries that skip partition keys may regress without index review.

currentProjects.title: Real-time fraud-rule auto-tuning
currentProjects.description: Replacing manual rule-threshold tweaks with an offline learner that proposes new thresholds from the last 14 days of chargebacks. Human-in-the-loop approval before deploy.
currentProjects.problem: Fraud thresholds are still tuned manually after chargeback review, which delays reaction time and creates inconsistent rule changes across markets.
approach: Build an offline learner that proposes threshold changes from chargeback, approval, and false-positive history.
Route every proposed change through human approval before production rollout.
Store rationale, expected lift, and rollback criteria with every rule proposal.
successMetrics: Reduce manual threshold review time by 40%
Improve chargeback precision without increasing false positives
Every auto-tuned rule has an approval trail
milestones: Define training dataset and guardrail metrics
Add approval workflow to risk console
Run two-market pilot before global rollout
risks: Model recommendations may overfit recent chargeback patterns.
Risk reviewers need enough explanation to trust proposed changes.

currentProjects.title: Webhook router consolidation
currentProjects.description: Merging three legacy webhook handlers (Stripe, Adyen, in-house) behind one event-bus consumer with idempotency-key replay protection.
currentProjects.problem: Stripe, Adyen, and in-house webhook handlers all implement retries and idempotency differently, making replay incidents hard to reason about.
approach: Move provider-specific handlers behind one event-bus consumer contract.
Normalize idempotency keys before events enter downstream payment services.
Add replay tooling so incident responders can safely reprocess provider events.
successMetrics: One webhook ingestion path for all providers
Duplicate charge attempts remain at zero during replay tests
Incident replay runbook validated by on-call engineers
milestones: Move Adyen and in-house providers after parity checks
risks: Provider-specific retry behavior may leak through the shared abstraction.
Replay tooling needs strict permissions to avoid unsafe production use.

name: Data Infrastructure
mission: The team that keeps the company's analytics + ML pipelines fed. We move billions of events per day from product systems into the warehouse, run the offline feature store, and operate the model-serving layer the recommendations + risk teams build on.

currentProjects.title: Feature-store v2 migration
currentProjects.description: Cutting over from the legacy in-house feature store to a Tecton-style serving layer with point-in-time correctness for training/inference parity.
currentProjects.problem: The legacy feature store makes it hard to guarantee point-in-time correctness between model training and online inference.
approach: Introduce a versioned feature registry with owners, freshness SLOs, and backfill policy.
Dual-write high-value features before moving model consumers.
Add parity checks between offline and online feature values for every migrated feature.
successMetrics: All tier-1 models read from feature-store v2
Feature parity checks stay above 99.5%
No model rollback caused by missing or stale features
milestones: Migrate fraud and recommendation features first
Cut online serving traffic by model group
risks: Dual-write windows can hide silent drift between stores.
Model teams may need help updating old feature definitions.

currentProjects.title: LLM eval harness for product surfaces
currentProjects.description: A shared offline eval pipeline so every team shipping an LLM feature (search, summarization, fraud explanations) gets calibrated regression scores, not vibes.
currentProjects.problem: Teams shipping LLM features lack a shared regression harness, so quality changes are judged manually and inconsistently.
approach: Create a standard eval dataset format with task, expected behavior, and failure category.
Run eval suites in CI for prompt and retrieval changes.
Expose trend dashboards so product teams can see quality movement over time.
successMetrics: Every LLM feature has at least one regression suite
Prompt changes show pass-rate deltas before merge
Teams can inspect failures without reading raw model logs
milestones: Add CI runner for one pilot team
Roll out to search and summarization surfaces
risks: Eval datasets may become stale if product behavior changes quickly.
Over-indexing on pass rate can miss qualitative regressions.

skillGaps: Frontend tooling for internal data UIs

name: Platform Experience
mission: Make the engineering platform feel obvious. We build the internal CLIs, dashboards, and CI scaffolding every other team touches daily — measured on how rarely you have to think about us.

currentProjects.title: Service template generator
currentProjects.description: A `og service new` CLI that scaffolds a service with logging, metrics, tracing, CI, and a working /healthz route — wired to the platform's golden defaults.
currentProjects.problem: New services take too long to start because each team recreates logging, health checks, tracing, and CI setup by hand.
approach: Generate a service skeleton with standard observability, Docker, CI, and ownership metadata.
Keep templates versioned so teams can safely adopt platform changes later.
Collect feedback from first services created through the CLI.
successMetrics: New service scaffold time under 10 minutes
90% of generated services pass platform checks on first run
Template upgrade path documented for existing services
milestones: Integrate ownership metadata with service catalog
Run feedback sessions with pilot teams
risks: Templates can drift from real production needs if platform owners do not maintain them.
Too many options in the CLI could make the default path less obvious.

currentProjects.title: Internal portal redesign
currentProjects.description: Rewriting the internal developer portal in Next.js with a design-system audit so service catalogs, runbooks, and on-call rotations live in one place.
currentProjects.problem: Runbooks, service metadata, on-call rotations, and ownership docs are spread across tools, slowing down daily engineering workflows.
approach: Rebuild the portal around service ownership as the primary navigation model.
Use the product design system so internal tools feel consistent.
Add search across runbooks, service metadata, and team ownership pages.
successMetrics: Engineers can find an owned service in under 20 seconds
Portal search returns a useful result for 70% of pilot queries
On-call links and runbooks are complete for tier-1 services
milestones: Run pilot with Platform Experience and Fraud Platform
risks: Old metadata may be incomplete or stale during migration.
Search relevance depends on teams cleaning up ownership records.

name: Payments Architecture
mission: The cross-team architecture guild for everything that moves money. We own the long-horizon roadmap — multi-region failover, payment-processor abstraction, fraud-pipeline observability — and the RFC process that keeps Fraud Platform, Risk, and Ledger pulling the same direction.

currentProjects.title: Multi-region payment-router design
currentProjects.description: RFC + reference implementation for a region-aware payment router that fails over within 30s of a regional Stripe outage, with deterministic idempotency-key handling across regions so duplicate retries don't re-charge.
currentProjects.problem: A regional processor outage can still force manual routing decisions, and retries must remain idempotent across failover paths.
approach: Define the region-aware routing contract and processor health model.
Prototype deterministic idempotency-key handling across regions.
Run failure drills against sandbox processors before implementation.
successMetrics: Failover target under 30 seconds in drill conditions
No duplicate charges during cross-region retry tests
RFC approved by Fraud, Risk, and Ledger representatives
milestones: Hand implementation plan to Fraud Platform
risks: Processor behavior differs enough to complicate one routing abstraction.
Failover automation needs clear manual override controls.

currentProjects.title: Fraud-pipeline observability spec
currentProjects.description: Cross-team observability standard for the end-to-end fraud-scoring path: trace-id propagation through webhook → router → rules-engine, p99 latency budgets per hop, and a shared Grafana board owned by this team.
currentProjects.problem: Fraud investigations require stitching together logs across webhook routing, fraud rules, transaction APIs, and chargeback ingestion.
approach: Standardize trace-id propagation from webhook receipt through fraud scoring and payment decisions.
Define p99 latency budgets per hop and publish them in a shared Grafana board.
Add required fields for fraud decisions so support and risk teams can reconstruct outcomes.
successMetrics: Every fraud decision has an end-to-end trace id
Shared dashboard covers all tier-1 fraud services
Incident triage time drops by 25% in rehearsal
milestones: Adopt spec in webhook-router and fraud-rules-engine
risks: Some legacy services may need instrumentation work before they can comply.
Too many required fields could slow adoption.

name: Security Platform
mission: Make our web surfaces hard to attack and easy to keep that way. We own client-side security posture — strict CSP, supply-chain integrity, auth-cookie hardening — and the frontend security review process that keeps adjacent teams from re-introducing the same classes of bugs.

currentProjects.title: Strict CSP rollout — internal admin
currentProjects.description: Extending the strict nonce-based CSP from user-facing surfaces to the internal admin tooling. Coordinating with platform-experience on the dev-portal migration and with auth-team on the admin sign-in flow.
currentProjects.problem: Internal admin tooling still permits script patterns that would be blocked on customer-facing surfaces, creating a weaker security posture for privileged workflows.
approach: Move admin pages to nonce-based CSP with a reporting-only warmup period.
Coordinate with auth and platform teams to replace inline scripts.
Track violations by owner before switching enforcement on.
successMetrics: CSP enforcement enabled for admin routes
No critical admin workflow blocked after rollout
All inline-script violations assigned to owners
milestones: Switch enforcement on by route group
risks: Third-party widgets may require exceptions that weaken the policy.
Admin teams need time to fix violations before enforcement.

currentProjects.title: Third-party-script supply-chain audit
currentProjects.description: Quarterly review of every pinned third-party script: SRI hash drift, vendor security posture, CDN ownership. Includes the build-step that auto-emits SRI hashes and the cron that alerts on drift.
currentProjects.problem: Third-party scripts can change ownership or content over time, and manual review does not catch SRI drift quickly enough.
approach: Inventory every third-party script and map it to a business owner.
Automate SRI hash generation and drift detection in CI.
Create a quarterly review checklist for vendor posture and CDN ownership.
successMetrics: 100% of third-party scripts have owners
SRI drift alerts within one day
Quarterly audit package ready for security review
risks: Some scripts may not support stable SRI workflows.
Business owners may not know why a script is still needed.
